dategroup — Date grouping tools¶

New in version 0.1.

Module author: Claude Gibert <claude.gibert@synopticview.co.uk>

The DateGroup class¶

class semperpy.core.dategroup.DateGroup(object)¶

The DateGroup class is an interface to different actors which are able to group dates accoring to different criteria. Actors are also implemented in this module:

from semperpy.core.date import Dates
from semperpy.core.dategroup import DateGroup

def displayData(groups):
    for group in groups.iterator():
        r = []
        for date,value in group:
            r.append(str(date) + ' ' + str(value))
        print ', '.join(r)

d = Dates(2011010100,2011010112) + Dates(2011010200,2011010212)
g = DateGroup.createGroup('hour',d)
displayData(g)

2011010100 None, 2011010200 None
2011010101 None, 2011010201 None
2011010102 None, 2011010202 None
2011010103 None, 2011010203 None
2011010104 None, 2011010204 None
2011010105 None, 2011010205 None
2011010106 None, 2011010206 None
2011010107 None, 2011010207 None
2011010108 None, 2011010208 None
2011010109 None, 2011010209 None
2011010110 None, 2011010210 None
2011010111 None, 2011010211 None
2011010112 None, 2011010212 None

Please ignore None for now. The grouping hour groups together all dates having the same hour independently of the the day, month or year. This is different from:

d = Dates(2011010100,2011010112) + Dates(2011010200,2011010212)
g = DateGroup.createGroup('hourly',d)
displayData(g)

2011010100 None
2011010101 None
2011010102 None
2011010103 None
2011010104 None
2011010105 None
2011010106 None
2011010107 None
2011010108 None
2011010109 None
2011010110 None
2011010111 None
2011010112 None
2011010200 None
2011010201 None
2011010202 None
2011010203 None
2011010204 None
2011010205 None
2011010206 None
2011010207 None
2011010208 None
2011010209 None
2011010210 None
2011010211 None
2011010212 None

In this case, the grouping doesn’t create any groups because hourly means having the same hour but also the same day, month year. A parameter can be added, the number of units of grouping (the number of hours here):

d = Dates(2011010100,2011010112) + Dates(2011010200,2011010212)
g = DateGroup.createGroup('hourly',d,3)
displayData(g)

2011010100 None, 2011010101 None, 2011010102 None
2011010103 None, 2011010104 None, 2011010105 None
2011010106 None, 2011010107 None, 2011010108 None
2011010109 None, 2011010110 None, 2011010111 None
2011010112 None
2011010200 None, 2011010201 None, 2011010202 None
2011010203 None, 2011010204 None, 2011010205 None
2011010206 None, 2011010207 None, 2011010208 None
2011010209 None, 2011010210 None, 2011010211 None
2011010212 None

The data is now grouped by chunks of 3 hours. The data can be grouped:

  • per hour (hour)
  • hourly (hourly,N)
  • per day (day)
  • daily (daily,N)
  • per month (month)
  • monthly (monthly,N)
  • per year (year)
  • yearly (yearly,N)

This grouping is designed to help with iterating over dates when some meaningful organisation is needed:

data = {}
d = Dates(2011010100,2011010112) + Dates(2011010200,2011010212)
for i in range(len(d)):
    data[d[i]] = i + 1
g = DateGroup.createGroup('hourly',data,3)
displayData(g)
print
g.add(2011010213,99)
displayData(g)

2011010100 1, 2011010101 2, 2011010102 3
2011010103 4, 2011010104 5, 2011010105 6
2011010106 7, 2011010107 8, 2011010108 9
2011010109 10, 2011010110 11, 2011010111 12
2011010112 13
2011010200 14, 2011010201 15, 2011010202 16
2011010203 17, 2011010204 18, 2011010205 19
2011010206 20, 2011010207 21, 2011010208 22
2011010209 23, 2011010210 24, 2011010211 25
2011010212 26

2011010100 1, 2011010101 2, 2011010102 3
2011010103 4, 2011010104 5, 2011010105 6
2011010106 7, 2011010107 8, 2011010108 9
2011010109 10, 2011010110 11, 2011010111 12
2011010112 13
2011010200 14, 2011010201 15, 2011010202 16
2011010203 17, 2011010204 18, 2011010205 19
2011010206 20, 2011010207 21, 2011010208 22
2011010209 23, 2011010210 24, 2011010211 25
2011010212 26, 2011010213 99

An example of application of this is when staging data from an archive. Files are stored in an archive, each file corresponds to data for a particular date-time. You suspect that all files from the same day are probably on the same tape and you know that it is safe to stage a whole day. You organise all the files involved in a dictionary which keys are the date and the value is the filename. You group the dictionary daily and write the loop:

for group in groups.iterator():
    files = group.values()
    stage(files)
    for date,filename in group:
        # do whatever

This way if you find that grouping the data 6 hourly or even 12 hourly, there is no need to change anything in the algorithm.

classmethod createGroup(name, container = None, count = 1)¶
Parameters:
  • name (string) – name of the grouping method
  • container (list of dict) – a list of dates or a { date: data }
  • count (int) – the number of basic group units

This method returns a DateGroup instance. The container can either be a list of date or a dictionary which keys are dates and values can be or any arbitrary type.

iterator()¶

Returns an iterators on the groups which attempts to iterate chronologically. For each iteration, an iterator is returned. That iterator returns tuples (date,value) for each couple present in a group by chronological order:

d = Dates(2011010100,2011010112) + Dates(2011010200,2011010212)
g = DateGroup.createGroup('hourlyGroup',d,6)
for group in g.iterator():
    for date, value in group:
        # do something
    # do something you want to do for each group of 6 hours.

The iterator returned for each group has also two methods which are dictionary-like:

  • keys(): returns a sorted list all keys
  • values(): returns a list of all values in the order of sorted keys.
